prompt:
Orchestration team ticket. Our Node workers kick off heavy Snowflake statements (history compaction, partition-stat rebuilds) and today they fire-and-forget: when a run blows its slot budget we just abandon the worker and the warehouse keeps burning credits for another 40 minutes. I want a proper asynchronous statement controller in TypeScript — stdlib only (node:http/fetch), no npm deps, no Snowflake SDK — that submits, polls, and actually CANCELS server-side when we say stop.

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Snowflake SQL API docs on docs.snowflake.com — at minimum the SQL API reference, the cancelling-requests page, and the handling-responses page. Confirm from the vendor's pages how async submission, status polling and the cancel endpoint actually work, including the cancel identity (code/sqlState) Snowflake reports. Our pinned wire contract is docs/contract.json, provenance docs/official_sources.json — protected fixtures, as is test_snowflake_cancel.ts. If live docs contradict a pin, call that out in your summary and leave fixtures/tests alone. Cite the exact official URLs you read in your final message.

Deliverable: `statement_controller.ts` as the entry module (split internals into more files as you see fit — I'd expect transport/controller/errors, 3+ source files, with the entry re-exporting). Exact surface is pinned by the tests:

- new StatementController({baseUrl, token, tokenType, userAgent, requestIds, now, wait}). Every request carries bearer auth, the token-type header, Accept and OUR User-Agent. requestIds hands out requestId values; now/wait are the only time sources — never Date.now or setTimeout.
- execute(statement, {deadlineMs?, signal?}) submits with async=true plus a fresh requestId, optional body fields omitted when unset, then polls EXACTLY the statementStatusUrl the server returned (query string included) at the pinned interval, waiting via wait(ms, signal).
- Cancellation: the deadline (now() vs deadlineMs) is checked only before scheduling each wait; the abort signal is checked before each wait and again when it returns. On either trigger, POST the documented cancel endpoint for the handle with a FRESH requestId, then verify the terminal state with one more status GET and report what Snowflake said — kind 'cancelled' with code/sqlState/message/statementHandle preserved. A poll that comes back 200 always wins: never cancel after a terminal success, even past the deadline.
- Outcomes: 'succeeded' carries statementHandle/code/numRows/rows (values verbatim strings, SQL NULL as null); a real SQL failure throws StatementFailedError with code/sqlState/message/statementHandle; other HTTP failures throw SqlApiError with status. No error text may ever contain the token.

Acceptance: `node --test test_snowflake_cancel.ts` (loopback fake, dummy creds, injected clock). Don't touch the test file or docs/.

research.interface: Snowflake SQL API v2, POST /api/v2/statements?async=true + GET /api/v2/statements/{statementHandle} + POST /api/v2/statements/{statementHandle}/cancel (GA)

official_sources.title: Snowflake SQL API reference
official_sources.used_for: the three statements endpoints including POST /api/v2/statements/{statementHandle}/cancel; requestId/async query parameters; QueryStatus (code 333334), QueryFailureStatus and CancelStatus schemas; canceled-statement identity code 000604 with sqlState 57014; status codes 200/202/401/422/429

official_sources.title: Canceling the execution of a SQL statement
official_sources.used_for: cancellation is a POST to the cancel endpoint addressed by statementHandle; the handle for an in-flight statement comes from the QueryStatus body

official_sources.title: Handling responses
official_sources.used_for: 202 QueryStatus with statementHandle/statementStatusUrl; polling GET /api/v2/statements/{handle} until a terminal 200 or 422; ResultSet rows encoded as strings with JSON null for SQL NULL

official_sources.title: Submitting a request to execute SQL statements
official_sources.used_for: async=true submission semantics, requestId UUID query parameter, required Authorization/Content-Type/Accept/User-Agent headers, request body fields statement/timeout/database/schema/warehouse/role

official_sources.title: Authenticating to the server
official_sources.used_for: Authorization: Bearer plus X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type header values KEYPAIR_JWT/OAUTH/PROGRAMMATIC_ACCESS_TOKEN

research.language_rationale: Our orchestration workers are Node services; Snowflake documents the SQL API precisely for such driverless integrations, and the Node SDK targets long-lived sessions rather than fire-and-cancel statement control. TypeScript with the built-in fetch/http stack is the idiomatic zero-dependency surface here.
